free_text: Exploring the alpha cluster structure of nuclei using
the thick target inverse kinematics technique for
multiple alpha decays
Experiment performed at the K150
cyclotron at Texas A&M University.
Gaseous 4He filled in reaction chamber with pressure
735/3800 bar and length of 48 cm was used as target.
Target thickness was sufficient to stop the beam few
centimeters before the detectors.
The energy of the light reaction
prodcts was measured by three silicon detector
telescopes placed at a radial distance of 48 cm from
the entrance window. Each telescope consisted of two
5x5 cm2 Micron Semiconductors DC quadrant detectors.
The thickness of the silicon detectors in the
telescopes was: telescope T0 1 mm/2 mm, T1 1 mm/2 mm
and T2 140 mu-m/300 mu-m.
Light particles were identified by E-dE method.
Thick target inverse kinematics was used to study
elastic scattering as well as multiple alpha particle
decays. This technique is particularly suited for
this study because it allows the exploration of a
large range of incident energies in the same
experiment. Moreover, in the inverse kinematics, the
reaction products are focused at forward angles and
can be detected with detectors covering a relatively
small portion of the solid angle in the forward
direction.
Special reconstruction code was developed to
reconstruct interaction point inside of thick gaseous
target, energy of the incident particle at interaction
point and angle of emitted particle . It allows
calculation of the interaction point inside the gas
volume, using the reaction kinematics, the measured
alpha particle energies and the range energy tables
from SRIM code. Time of flight information was used
to double check the position of the interaction point.
Compiled by S.H.
Initial beam energy was 2.9 AMeV
Data presented in fig. 2 sent by author (M.B.)
Initial beam energy was 9.7 AMeV
